planting.note: Plant balled-and-burlapped or container stock in spring or early fall while soil is workable.

directions: Set this narrow, columnar juniper in full sun and well-drained soil; it tolerates poor, dry, and rocky ground once established. Space plants at least 5 feet apart when used as a screen so the slender pyramidal form fills in without crowding. Water regularly the first year to establish deep roots, then reduce watering as it becomes quite drought-tolerant. Its dense evergreen foliage holds green year-round, making it a reliable windbreak or accent needing almost no pruning.

planting.note: Plant after the last hard frost; in colder zones grow in a container that can be moved to shelter.

directions: This cold-hardy fig tolerates temperatures near 10 degrees F, making it a good choice for cooler regions where most figs struggle. Plant in full sun with rich, well-drained soil and provide consistent moisture while fruit is developing for deep burgundy fruit with a pink center. In borderline zones, mulch heavily around the base or grow in a pot to overwinter in a protected spot. Prune in late winter to keep the tree at a manageable height and encourage a strong branching structure.

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container trees in early spring; pair with another Japanese or American plum for cross-pollination.

directions: This cold-hardy hybrid plum produces juicy reddish-purple fruit with a distinctive sweet, spicy flavor often compared to bubblegum. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil, and include a compatible pollinizer nearby since Toka also serves as an excellent pollinator for other plums. Water consistently during fruit set and thin heavy fruit clusters to improve size and prevent limb breakage. Prune in late winter to an open-center form for good light penetration and air flow.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall in a site with room for a slow-growing specimen conifer.

directions: This slow-growing spruce features weeping, lace-like branches clothed in soft blue-green needles for an elegant textured specimen. Plant in full sun and cool, moist, well-drained soil; it appreciates protection from harsh, drying winds. Keep the root zone mulched and evenly moist, especially while young, as Engelmann spruce dislikes prolonged drought. Little pruning is needed beyond removing damaged branches, letting the graceful cascading habit develop naturally.

planting.note: Plant rhizomes or divisions in spring or early fall with the crown just below the soil surface.

directions: This Siberian iris opens bicolor blooms of soft butter-yellow falls and crisp white standards atop slender, grassy foliage. Plant in full sun to light shade in moist, humus-rich soil; it tolerates wetter ground better than bearded iris. Keep evenly watered during the spring bloom period and divide clumps every few years to maintain vigor. It is low-maintenance and reliably hardy, spreading into graceful clumps that make excellent border and waterside plantings.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in consistently moist, acidic soil.

directions: Japanese iris produces large, flat, ruffled blooms whose delicate petals resemble butterflies taking flight in late spring and summer. Plant in full sun to part shade in rich, acidic soil that stays consistently moist; it thrives at pond edges and in rain gardens. Never let the soil dry out during the growing season, and avoid lime. Divide congested clumps in early fall and remove spent flower stalks to keep plantings tidy.

planting.note: Plant tubers after all danger of frost has passed and the soil has warmed.

directions: This prolific dahlia shifts through the season from vivid pink and purple to a smoky mauve, giving weeks of color changes on strong stems. Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in full sun and rich, well-drained soil after frost, and pinch the growing tip early to encourage bushy, branching growth. Stake or corral tall plants and cut blooms often to keep flowers coming. In zones colder than 8, lift and store the tubers in a cool, dry place over winter.

planting.note: Grow outdoors only in frost-free zones; elsewhere keep in a container that overwinters indoors.

directions: This ornamental citrus, a kumquat-mandarin cross, bears a heavy crop of small, round, tart fruit prized for marmalades and drinks. Grow in full sun in well-drained, slightly acidic soil, and feed regularly with a citrus fertilizer during the growing season. In non-tropical climates keep it potted so it can move to a bright indoor spot before frost. Fragrant white blossoms and glossy evergreen leaves make it as decorative as it is productive.

planting.note: Plant outdoors in warm zones; grow in a movable container in colder regions.

directions: This compact mandarin produces sweet, juicy, easy-peeling fruit and stays small enough for patios or sunny indoor rooms. Plant in full sun in fast-draining soil and water deeply but allow the top inch to dry between waterings. Feed with a citrus-specific fertilizer through the growing season and protect from frost, moving containers indoors when temperatures drop. Light pruning after harvest keeps the canopy open and the tree productive.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall near a trellis, fence, or arbor for the vine to climb.

directions: This evergreen vine covers fences, trellises, and arbors with fragrant, bright yellow trumpet blooms in late winter to early spring. Plant in full sun to part shade in moist, well-drained soil and provide sturdy support for the twining stems. Water regularly while establishing, then it tolerates moderate drought once mature. Note that all parts of the plant are toxic if eaten, so site it away from areas frequented by pets and children; prune lightly after flowering to control size.

planting.note: Plant divisions in spring or early fall in rich, moist, acidic soil.

directions: Cascade Spice bears large, ruffled Japanese iris blooms in rich purple and white for a bold late-spring to summer display. Site in full sun to part shade in acidic, humus-rich soil kept consistently moist, ideal at the edge of a pond or in a rain garden. Do not allow the soil to dry out during active growth, and avoid alkaline amendments. Deadhead spent stalks and divide crowded clumps in fall to sustain strong flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost in a warm, sunny site with good drainage.

directions: This fast-growing crape myrtle covers itself in vivid purple flower panicles from summer into fall, followed by attractive fall foliage and exfoliating bark. Plant in full sun for the heaviest bloom in average, well-drained soil; too much shade reduces flowering. Water regularly while establishing, then it tolerates heat and moderate drought. Prune lightly in late winter to shape, removing crossing branches rather than topping the tree to preserve its natural form.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Center Court is an elegant creamy-white dahlia that blooms nonstop all season and produces tubers reliably, making it a great choice for beginners. Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in full sun and fertile, well-drained soil once the ground has warmed. Pinch young plants to promote branching and cut flowers regularly to prolong blooming. In cold-winter regions, dig and store the tubers indoors after the first frost blackens the foliage.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in a site with morning sun and afternoon shade in hot climates.

directions: Cherokee Brave opens deep pink spring blooms with reddish new foliage, followed by red fall color and berries that attract birds. Plant in moist, acidic, well-drained soil rich in organic matter, siting it in part shade or where it gets afternoon protection in hot regions. Keep the root zone mulched and evenly moist, as dogwoods are shallow-rooted and dislike drought. This selection shows improved resistance to powdery mildew; prune only to remove dead or crossing branches after flowering.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in a sheltered spot with dappled light in warm climates.

directions: Cherokee Chief bears rich rosy-red bracts in spring on graceful horizontal branches, with glossy foliage that turns red in autumn. Plant in acidic, humus-rich, well-drained soil and give it part shade or afternoon protection where summers are hot. Maintain even soil moisture with mulch, since the shallow roots suffer in dry conditions. Prune sparingly right after bloom to shape the canopy and remove any damaged wood.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in dappled light with cool, moist, acidic soil.

directions: Cherokee Princess is prized for its heavy set of large, pure-white spring bracts and a well-branched, rounded form. Plant in moist, acidic, organically rich soil in part shade, protecting it from harsh afternoon sun and drying wind. Mulch to keep the shallow root system cool and consistently moist. Enjoy red fall foliage and berries that feed birds; prune only lightly after flowering to maintain the tidy structure.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in morning sun with afternoon shade in hot climates.

directions: This bigleaf hydrangea produces color-shifting blooms that mature to rich cherry-red tones from spring into fall on a compact, rounded shrub. Plant in part shade with morning sun and afternoon protection in moist, well-drained, organically rich soil. Water deeply and regularly, especially in heat, and mulch to conserve moisture. It blooms on both old and new wood, so prune only to remove dead stems in early spring for the most reliable flowering.

planting.note: Plant after the last frost; in cold zones the tops may die back and regrow from the roots.

directions: One of the hardiest figs available, Chicago Hardy can be grown down to Zone 5, where the tops may freeze back but resprout from the roots to fruit on new wood. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and mulch heavily over winter in colder regions to protect the crown. Water steadily while fruit develops for the best sweetness. In the coldest zones, grow it in a container that can overwinter in a garage or basement.

planting.note: Grow outdoors only in tropical zones; elsewhere keep as a container plant moved indoors for winter.

directions: This tropical evergreen yields true cinnamon, harvested from the fragrant inner bark of young stems. Grow in full sun to part shade in rich, well-drained soil kept consistently moist but never soggy. Outside frost-free regions, keep it potted and bring it into a warm, bright room before temperatures drop, since it is very frost-sensitive. Coppice mature stems to encourage the tender shoots that produce the best bark for harvesting.

planting.note: Set out after frost; grow in containers to overwinter indoors in cold climates.

directions: This fast-growing scented geranium has citrus-fragrant foliage and pretty pink blooms, thriving in hot, sunny gardens and patio pots. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil and let the top of the soil dry slightly between waterings. Pinch stems to keep it bushy and take cuttings easily to make more plants. It is frost-tender, so bring containers indoors to a bright window before the first freeze.

planting.note: Plant outdoors in warm zones; grow in a movable container elsewhere.

directions: Clementine produces sweet, seedless, easy-peeling mandarins on a tidy, manageable tree suitable for gardens or large containers. Plant in full sun in fast-draining, slightly acidic soil and water deeply, letting the surface dry between waterings to avoid root rot. Feed with a citrus fertilizer through spring and summer for strong fruit set. Protect from frost by moving potted trees indoors to a bright spot when cold weather arrives.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall against a sturdy wall, fence, or large tree it can cling to.

directions: This vigorous woody vine climbs walls and trees by aerial rootlets, covering surfaces with heart-shaped leaves and fragrant, lacy white blooms in late spring. Plant in part shade to full shade in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil; it also tolerates morning sun with steady moisture. Be patient, as it is slow to establish but grows rapidly once settled, eventually reaching great heights. Provide strong support and prune after flowering only to control size.

planting.note: Plant outdoors in warm zones; grow in a container that can move indoors in cooler regions.

directions: This semi-dwarf grapefruit-pomelo hybrid produces juicy, sweet-tart, low-acid fruit on a compact tree ideal for patios or small yards. Grow in full sun in fast-draining, slightly acidic soil and water deeply while letting the surface dry between waterings. Feed with a citrus fertilizer during the growing season for good fruit development. Move container trees to a bright indoor location before frost, as grapefruit is cold-sensitive.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost in a hot, sunny site with sharp drainage.

directions: This cold-tolerant pomegranate bears vibrant red-fruited crops and handsome orange-red flowers on a large, multi-stemmed shrub or small tree. Plant in full sun in well-drained soil; it thrives in heat and tolerates drought once established. Water deeply but infrequently, easing off as fruit ripens to prevent splitting. In the colder end of its range, site it against a warm south wall and mulch the base for winter protection.

planting.note: Plant in spring or early fall with plenty of room for the broad pyramidal form.

directions: This iconic conifer forms a stately pyramid of silvery-blue needles, serving as a striking specimen or windbreak. Plant in full sun in deep, moist, well-drained soil and give it space, as mature trees grow large. Water regularly while young to establish deep roots, then it tolerates cold and moderate drought well. Avoid crowding to reduce needle-cast and canker problems, and prune only to remove dead or damaged branches.

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container trees in early spring in a sunny, well-drained site.

directions: Contender is a disease-resistant, cold-hardy, self-pollinating peach that produces abundant medium-to-large juicy freestone fruit across many climates. Plant in full sun in fertile, well-drained soil and water consistently during fruit development. Thin young fruit to about 6 inches apart to improve size and prevent limb breakage. Prune annually in late winter to an open-center shape for light penetration, and apply dormant sprays as needed to manage common peach diseases.

planting.note: Plant in spring or fall in a sheltered site with protection from hot afternoon sun.

directions: Prized for its glowing coral-red winter bark, this Japanese maple also offers soft green spring foliage that shifts to golden-yellow in fall. Plant in part shade with rich, moist, well-drained soil, protecting it from drying wind and intense afternoon sun that can scorch leaves. Keep the root zone mulched and evenly moist. Prune minimally in late winter to reveal the colorful stems and maintain the graceful branching form.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, well-drained soil and stake tall plants.

directions: Coralie blends the most perfect blush and pink shades, pairing beautifully with other soft-toned flowers in the cutting garden. Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in full sun and fertile, well-drained soil after the ground warms. Because the plant grows tall, provide sturdy staking or support unless you are cutting frequently. Pinch early for branching, cut often to keep blooms coming, and lift tubers to store indoors over winter in cold zones.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Cornel is the classic burnt-red ball dahlia, a shorter, sturdy plant that often needs no staking and blooms prolifically. Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in full sun and fertile, well-drained soil once the soil has warmed. Pinch the growing tips to encourage branching and cut the tightly formed blooms often for long-lasting arrangements. In cold-winter regions, dig and store the tubers, which this variety produces reliably, after the first frost.

planting.note: Plant tubers after frost in warm, well-drained soil.

directions: Cornel Bronze is a burnt-orange ball dahlia related to Cornel, sharing its prolific bloom, excellent tuber production, and tidy plant habit. Plant tubers 4 to 6 inches deep in full sun and rich, well-drained soil after the last frost. Pinch young plants for bushier growth and harvest the round, tightly petaled blooms frequently for the vase. Lift and store the abundant tubers indoors over winter in regions colder than Zone 8.

planting.note: Broadcast seed on warm soil after frost; it grows fast and is easily killed by cold.

directions: Buckwheat is a fast-growing warm-season cover crop that smothers weeds, attracts pollinators, and improves soil when turned under. Broadcast seed densely onto raked soil after the last frost and rake in lightly; it germinates within a few days. Mow or till it in at flowering, before seeds set, to prevent unwanted reseeding and to add organic matter. It thrives in poor soils and needs little water, making it an easy short-term summer cover between crops.

planting.note: Sow in late summer to fall for overwintering, or early spring in cold regions.

directions: Crimson clover is a nitrogen-fixing legume cover crop that enriches soil and produces showy crimson flowers loved by bees. Broadcast seed onto prepared soil in fall for overwintering or in early spring, raking in lightly for good contact. Let it grow to bloom, then mow and turn it under a few weeks before planting to release nitrogen and add organic matter. Distinct from perennial red clover, it grows quickly and is easy to incorporate into rotations.

planting.note: Plant individual cloves in fall a few weeks before the ground freezes for a summer harvest.

directions: This heat-tolerant Creole-type garlic has bright pinkish-red wrappers and yields the largest bulbs in mild-winter regions, with a distinctive shallot-like flavor. Plant single cloves pointed-end up, 2 inches deep and 4 to 6 inches apart, in fall in loose, fertile, well-drained soil. Mulch after planting, keep evenly watered in spring, and stop watering as the lower leaves brown. Harvest when several lower leaves have died back, then cure the bulbs in a dry, airy spot before storing.

planting.note: Plant bare-root or container trees in early spring with a compatible pear nearby for pollination.

directions: D'Anjou is a vigorous, productive European pear yielding sweet, juicy fruit excellent for fresh eating, baking, and canning. Plant in full sun in deep, well-drained soil and include a different pear variety nearby for cross-pollination. Water consistently during fruit development and prune in late winter to a central-leader form. Harvest the fruit while still firm and ripen it off the tree in cool storage for the best texture.
